texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract The economy-wide production network, i.e., the monetary input-output coefficients, will no longer be stable against the general equilibrium propagation of sectoral productivity shocks, when the substitution elasticities of sectoral production are nonneutral. The associated network transformation hence renders nonlinearity in aggregating sectoral productivity shocks into the economy's output, i.e., Domar aggregation. In this study, we demonstrate the possibility of production networks being transformed into a singular one. We also explore the conditions under which the productivity shocks experience synergism in Domar aggregation.
texts.text: Abstract The economy-wide production network, i.e., the monetary input-output coefficients, will no longer be stable against the general equilibrium propagation of sectoral productivity shocks, when the substitution elasticities of sectoral production are nonneutral. The associated network transformation hence renders nonlinearity in aggregating sectoral productivity shocks into the economy's output, i.e., Domar aggregation. In this study, we demonstrate the possibility of production networks being transformed into a singular one. We also explore the conditions under which the productivity shocks experience synergism in Domar aggregation.

texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Suppose all industrial sectors of the entire economy reveal constant returns to scale. Then, for each sector, the unit cost function with pregiven level of productivity becomes scale-free and only dependent on factor prices. Owing to the fact that all commodities are made by means of commodities, while assuming all commodities are produced by different but unique sectors (i.e., no joint production) and that there is only one kind of primary factor not made by means of commodities, the prices of commodities relative to the primary factor are determined as the solution to the general equilibrium system of equations that equilibrate commodity prices to unit costs of sectors. Shephard's lemma nonetheless indicates that input-output coefficients will only be affected by the equilibrium price change, which will only be triggered by the propagation of productivity shocks across all sectors. 1
texts.text: Suppose all industrial sectors of the entire economy reveal constant returns to scale. Then, for each sector, the unit cost function with pregiven level of productivity becomes scale-free and only dependent on factor prices. Owing to the fact that all commodities are made by means of commodities, while assuming all commodities are produced by different but unique sectors (i.e., no joint production) and that there is only one kind of primary factor not made by means of commodities, the prices of commodities relative to the primary factor are determined as the solution to the general equilibrium system of equations that equilibrate commodity prices to unit costs of sectors. Shephard's lemma nonetheless indicates that input-output coefficients will only be affected by the equilibrium price change, which will only be triggered by the propagation of productivity shocks across all sectors. 1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The idea of aggregating productivity growths associated with industrial sectors into national aggregate output growth measures is called the Domar aggregation. In a closed economy, aggregate output growth in the above setting essentially equals the aggregated commodity prices via the representative household's indirect utility, where the commodity prices are determined by the sectoral unit cost functions with productivity growths exogenously given. Hulten (1978) claimed that sectoral productivity growths can be mapped into national aggregate output growth by linear aggregation using Domar weights. Linear aggregation of sectoral productivity growths, however, is only possible in an economy where the representative household and all the production sectors aggregate commodities in a Cobb-Douglas fashion, so that the monetary input-output coefficients remain constant. 2
texts.text: The idea of aggregating productivity growths associated with industrial sectors into national aggregate output growth measures is called the Domar aggregation. In a closed economy, aggregate output growth in the above setting essentially equals the aggregated commodity prices via the representative household's indirect utility, where the commodity prices are determined by the sectoral unit cost functions with productivity growths exogenously given. Hulten (1978) claimed that sectoral productivity growths can be mapped into national aggregate output growth by linear aggregation using Domar weights. Linear aggregation of sectoral productivity growths, however, is only possible in an economy where the representative household and all the production sectors aggregate commodities in a Cobb-Douglas fashion, so that the monetary input-output coefficients remain constant. 2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Accordingly, Acemoglu et al (2012, 2017); Acemoglu and Azar (2020) studied Domar aggregation under Cobb-Douglas technology where Domar weights are the product of representative household's expenditure shares and the (monetary) Leontief inverse. In this case, the volatility of the aggregate output fluctuations will contract disproportionally to the number of sectors whose productivity shocks are being aggregated, owing to the granular property (Gabaix, 2011) of the Leontief inverse. Baqaee and Farhi (2019); Nakano and Nishimura (2021) then studied aggregate fluctuations of an economy comprising non-Cobb-Douglas technology and showed that aggregate fluctuations will have nonnormal tails even if sectoral productivity shocks have normal tails, owing to the nonlinear nature of Domar aggregation that can potentially induce network transformation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From empirical perspective, Kim et al (2017); Nakano and Nishimura (2024) examined economy-wide propagation of innovation, where innovation is proxied by productivity shocks and their propagation is concerned across the production network of sectors with empirically estimated CES (constant elasticity of substitution) unit cost functions. In this setting, the fixed point solution to the general equilibrium system of equations is solved by way of recursive mapping from factor prices onto commodity unit costs which will globally converge to a unique equilibrium, owing to the concavity of the mapping, if only the solution existed in the positive-finite domain. During the course of analyses, however, there were cases (of productivity shocks) where the recursive mapping experienced nonconvergence, indicating the absence of the solution in the positive-finite domain.

In this study we hence explore the role of substitution elasticities in regard to the emergence of network singularity and synergism in nonlinear Domar aggregation. 3 In so doing, we work on CES production networks with a universal substitution elasticity σ , where the system of unit cost functions becomes linear with respect to the transcendent variables, i.e., variables transformed by the power of (1 -σ ) th order. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In the follwing section we present the benchmark general equilibrium system of equations encompassing multifactor CES unit cost functions with single (universal) substitution elasticity, and derive the Domar aggregation function with respect to the productivity shocks. The universal substitution elasticity setting with the use of transcendent variables allows us to handle general equilibrium solutions by means of linear algebra. In Section 3, we discuss network singularity and specify the conditions under which such situation can actually take place. We find that an inelastic (elastic) network may encounter a negative (positive) singularity. In Section 4, we discuss synergy in the economic evaluation of two different innovations. We find that an inelastic (elastic) network entails negative (positive) synergism. Section 5 concludes the paper.

In Section 3, we discuss network singularity and specify the conditions under which such situation can actually take place. We find that an inelastic (elastic) network may encounter a negative (positive) singularity. In Section 4, we discuss synergy in the economic evaluation of two different innovations. We find that an inelastic (elastic) network entails negative (positive) synergism. Section 5 concludes the paper.

texts.orig: For later convenience, let us call a production network subject to substitution elasticity less than unity ( σ < 1 ), an inelastic network , and that subject to substitution elasticity greater than unity ( σ > 1 ), an elastic network . A neutroelastic network ( σ = 1 ) is otherwise called a Cobb-Douglas production network. Both inelastic and elastic networks are subject to transformation given sectoral productivity shocks, whereas neutroelastic network is not.

texts.orig: Formula (12) indicates that Domar aggregation is linear as long as the elasticity of substitution across the production network is neutral. Note that the column vector [ I -A ] -1 κ corresponds to the Domar weights. The linearity stems from the robustness of the production network against sectoral productivity shocks under neutral substitution elasticity. In contrast, formula (13) indicates that Domar aggregation is nonlinear if the elasticity of substitution across the production network is nonneutral. The nonlinearity stems from the fragility of the production network against sectoral productivity shocks under nonneutral substitution elasticity. Fallacy of composition (or, synergism) will never take place for linear Domar aggregation that does not evoke network transformation, whereas it will take place for nonlinear Domar aggregation that brings about network transfromation.

texts.orig: 5 Concluding Remarks
texts.text: 5 Concluding Remarks

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to Eden et al (2012), singularity hypotheses refer to two distinct scenarios. One is the intelligence explosion (Good, 1966), whereby machine intelligence surpasses human intelligence through computational advancement, which entails recursive self-improvement. The other scenario is the biointelligence explosion, which entails the amplification of human capabilities to overcome all existing human limitations (Kurzweil, 2005). In both ways, however, the concept of singularity (i.e., explosion) does not go beyond exponential growth, if not extreme. The economic singularity theory of Nordhaus (2021) also exploits the idea of extreme exponential growth of intelligence capital deepening by a large elasticity of substitution. In contrast, the singularity presented in this study is based on matrix singularity where the matrix is transformed by virtue of the substitution elasticity that is nonneutral.
texts.text: According to Eden et al (2012), singularity hypotheses refer to two distinct scenarios. One is the intelligence explosion (Good, 1966), whereby machine intelligence surpasses human intelligence through computational advancement, which entails recursive self-improvement. The other scenario is the biointelligence explosion, which entails the amplification of human capabilities to overcome all existing human limitations (Kurzweil, 2005). In both ways, however, the concept of singularity (i.e., explosion) does not go beyond exponential growth, if not extreme. The economic singularity theory of Nordhaus (2021) also exploits the idea of extreme exponential growth of intelligence capital deepening by a large elasticity of substitution. In contrast, the singularity presented in this study is based on matrix singularity where the matrix is transformed by virtue of the substitution elasticity that is nonneutral.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Of course, positive singularity cannot take place unless some finite amount of non-labor commodity can completely substitute the primary factor of production. Our model allowed convergence towards positive singularity because our (standard) CES production function allowed production even at the extreme position of the isoquant near the axes. On the other hand, negative singularity is not an improbable event. With some productivity-diminishing innovations, such as those involving environmental conservation technologies, network transformation under inelastic network can encounter a state that some commodity cannot be produced in net. Finally, as regards the matter of industrial policy, whether to promote a portfolio of projects, or rather to take a selective policy, depends on the synergism inherent in the production network where the elasticity of substitution plays the important role.
